paragraphs.body: My name is , and I am a student at the university of Pittsburgh, I recently took the pleasure in reading, “ Are you allowed to criticize Simone Biles?: A Decision Tree,” which argued the idea that it is blatantly ignorant to criticize someone about a topic that you have absolutely no experience with. Right off the bat I noticed the fantastic use of satire in this back-and-forth style conversation between a conservative figure whose voice proves the sheer arrogance of the conservative media figures who verbally attacked Simone for pulling out of the women’s team competition. Some examples of this that really stood out to me include response to the posed question of whether or not this conservative figure had revolutionized their sport and invented “4 mindbogglingly complex moves” to which the following response being “No, I’m just a pasty middle-aged sad sack griping about “courage” because I’ve never encountered any real form of adversity in my life, and criticizing Black female athletes is my way of expressing deep-seated feelings of inferiority at my relative lack of talent.” I admire how the juxtaposed sort of way this mocking tone goes on to carefully illustrate the insecurities that are rooted in these conservative media figures and how they feel the need to take down others to feel better about themselves. At the end of this essay after a list of the adversities she faced including, growing up in foster care, being sexually abused by the team doctor, and years of neglect from the FBI regarding the abuse, she affirms that yes, she had prevailed over those challenges and become the greatest gymnast in the world. There is a palpable sense of validation in this section of the essay that only increased my sympathy at which is then cemented when the final message directed at not only Biles but also the audience comes to fruition, “You are the only the only person on Earth who is allowed to criticize you. But go on easy on yourself, girl. you did what you had to do.”

annotations.text: admire how the juxtaposed sort of way this mocking tone goes on to carefully illustrate the insecurities that are rooted in these conservative media figures and how they feel the need to take down others to feel better about themselves.
annotations.comment: Excellent work pointing to a concrete and specific passage from the text and providing feedback on the writing.

paragraphs.body: At some capacity I can relate to this, I have had my own challenges that I have dealt with in my life, a lot of them having to do with the way my neurodivergent brain operates, but also they way my family dynamic works. Ever since my mother left the scene six years ago and married another man, I became the only one under the Walsh family name to go to college and because of this my father expects a lot out of me. I hold it to myself to give my best in everything I do, but I also exert more effort into assignments than others would need to. Earlier this term I would have received a $3,000 grant if I had taken a fourth course which I had tried to do last term but it the workload was too much for my brain to process and I this ended up effecting my grades. So when I decided to stick to three courses my father said he understood but there clearly was a lot of disappointment because this meant the grant was out of the question. This left me feeling like a financial burden but also as though money was more important than my mental well-being. I was always and still criticized by my younger brother by two years, he had called me a loser, retard, idiot, any derogatory adjective he could think of. But at the same time I know that putting me down is how he copes with the knowledge that he is failing high school. So I continue to try to worry about myself and not the others who feel the need to take out their personal suffrage on me.

paragraphs.body: When initially writing this essay did you know the format you were going to use? Has writing for a young adult audience shifted some previous ideas on how you were going to write about these conservative media figures that had attacked Simone Biles? I enjoy how the questions toward the conservative figure progressively become more detailed until the end where the conservative figure almost seems to be forfeiting from the conversation in the line “No, I’m not Simone Biles, I’m just some dipshit” I think that’s the point where it is finally reiterates that the conservative figure never had any good argument to why they treated Biles in such a brutal way but just did it out of closed-mindedness and feelings of being inferior.

paragraphs.body: Thank you for sharing an essay that not only highlights Simone Biles being criticized by these conservative media figures but also the psychological aspects of why they attacked her. I will move forward knowing that when I am berated not to take it too close to heart and to consider the experiences of another person before speaking my thoughts onto them.

annotations.comment: Thank you so much for sharing these personal stories! You're doing an excellent job of making connections between the text and your own experiences and observations. I'm really looking forward to seeing what you do next.
